# Chapter 15 — Introduction to Convolutional Neural Networks

> **Goal:** understand what a CNN *is* by building its pieces — convolution,
> padding/stride, ReLU, max pooling — by hand in NumPy, and running one
> **forward pass** end to end. This is the **conceptual bridge** from the MLP of
> Chapter 13 to modern deep learning for images.

## Why this chapter

The neural network of Chapter 13 treats its input as a flat list of numbers.
For images that is wasteful and naive:

- **Parameter explosion** — flattening a $28\times 28$ image into an MLP's first
  $100$-neuron layer already needs $78{,}400$ weights; a small color photo needs
  millions.
- **Ignored spatial structure** — flattening throws away geometry, so the
  network cannot see that neighbouring pixels form edges, corners, and strokes.
- **No translation tolerance** — a shifted digit looks entirely new to an MLP.

A **convolutional neural network** fixes all three with one idea: use a *small*
kernel of shared weights and **slide** it across the image (**weight sharing**).

## Core ideas in this chapter

### Convolution (cross-correlation)

Slide a $k\times k$ **kernel** over the image; at each position take the dot
product of the kernel with the patch beneath it:

$$
\text{out}[i,j] = \sum_{u=0}^{k-1}\sum_{v=0}^{k-1}
\text{image}[i+u,\,j+v]\,\cdot\,\text{kernel}[u,v].
$$

Different kernels detect different patterns — **Sobel-x/Sobel-y** for edges,
an averaging kernel for **blur**, a center-boosting kernel for **sharpen**.

### Padding and stride

With padding $p$ and stride $s$, an input of side $H$ and kernel side $k$ gives
an output of side

$$
\text{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H - k + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1.
$$

*Valid* padding ($p=0$) shrinks the map; *same* padding keeps the size; a stride
$s>1$ downsamples.

### ReLU and max pooling

A convolution is linear, so we follow it with **ReLU**, $\max(0,z)$ (the same
activation as Chapter 13), applied elementwise. **Max pooling** then slides a
small window and keeps the largest value in each — shrinking the map and adding
**translation tolerance**.

### The pipeline (forward pass)

$$
\underbrace{[\,\text{conv}\to\text{ReLU}\to\text{pool}\,]}_{\text{repeat}}
\to \text{flatten} \to \text{fully-connected} \to \text{softmax}.
$$

We run one forward pass on a small image (random kernels are fine) and watch the
shapes shrink — $16\times16 \to 14 \to 7 \to 5 \to 2 \to$ a class-probability
vector that sums to $1$.

## Key things that surprise newcomers

| Thing | Note |
|-------|------|
| "Convolution" | in deep learning it's really **cross-correlation** (no kernel flip) |
| A kernel is tiny | $3\times3$ = $9$ shared weights, reused at every position |
| Why it beats an MLP | weight sharing + local patches = far fewer parameters |
| Valid padding shrinks | $9\times9$ with a $3\times3$ kernel → $7\times7$ |
| Max pooling | keeps the strongest response → translation tolerance |
| Real kernels are **learned** | here they're hand-made/random; training uses gradient descent |

## Scope

This is a **forward-pass** chapter. We do **not** train the convolution. Backprop
through a conv layer is the **same chain rule** as Chapter 13, with the one extra
wrinkle of **weight sharing** (a kernel's gradient sums over every position it
visited). That derivation is left for later study.

## After this chapter

You know what a convolutional network *is*: shared kernels slid across an image,
nonlinearity, pooling, and a familiar classifier on top. From here, libraries
like PyTorch and TensorFlow provide fast, trainable conv layers — but you now
understand what they compute underneath.

## 📚 Further reading

- Andrew Ng — [Deep Learning Specialization](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/deep-learning) (Coursera) — Course 4, "Convolutional Neural Networks," covers exactly this.
- [Deep Learning book](https://www.deeplearningbook.org) — Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville (free) — chapter 9 on convolutional networks.
